note: P4 cross-family assessor votes via OpenRouter (off-7-19). Measured, NOT a pinned 7/19 artifact. Wire into build_assessment.py + the kernel φ-flip post-freeze.

reasons.F1: Worobey et al. (2022) state that the earliest known December-2019 COVID-19 cases were geographically centered on the Huanan market, including cases with no reported direct market link.
reasons.F2: Worobey et al. (2022) report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
reasons.F3: Worobey et al. (2022) report that SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples inside the market were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.
reasons.F4: Worobey et al. (2022) state that there is insufficient evidence to define upstream events, and exact circumstances remain obscure.
reasons.F5: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) arising from at least two separate cross-species transmission events.
reasons.F6: Pekar et al. (2022) estimate the first (lineage B) zoonotic transmission occurred around 18 November 2019 (23 October to 8 December).
reasons.F7: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that it is unlikely that SARS-CoV-2 circulated widely in humans before November 2019.
reasons.F8: The corpus sources do not report direct genomic sequencing that identifies the specific intermediate animal host from which SARS-CoV-2 spilled over.
reasons.I1: Worobey et al. (2022) state that the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market in Wuhan was the early epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic.
reasons.I2: The available corpus evidence, including the findings of Worobey et al. (2022) and Pekar et al. (2022), supports zoonotic spillover as a probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2's emergence.
reasons.I3: The Evidence does not provide information about the statistical independence of the three Worobey proximity findings.
reasons.I4: The Pekar molecular two-lineage finding and Worobey's market-proximity findings appear to be based on distinct data.
reasons.I5: The Evidence does not provide a basis for reporting a single confident point-estimate odds ratio for zoonosis versus the alternative.
reasons.I6: The corpus evidence is not sufficient to definitively rule out any non-market introduction of SARS-CoV-2 into humans, as the exact circumstances remain obscure.

reasons.F1: Worobey et al. (2022) explicitly state that the earliest known December-2019 cases were geographically centered on the Huanan market, including cases with no reported direct market link.
reasons.F2: Worobey et al. (2022) report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
reasons.F3: Worobey et al. (2022) report that SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples inside the market were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.
reasons.F4: Worobey et al. (2022) state that there is insufficient evidence to define upstream events, and exact circumstances remain obscure.
reasons.F5: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) arising from at least two separate cross-species transmission events.
reasons.F6: Pekar et al. (2022) estimate the first (lineage B) zoonotic transmission occurred around mid-to-late November 2019.
reasons.F7: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that it is unlikely that SARS-CoV-2 circulated widely in humans before November 2019.
reasons.F8: The corpus sources do not report direct genomic sequencing that identifies the specific intermediate animal host from which SARS-CoV-2 spilled over.
reasons.I1: The evidence from Worobey et al. (2022) supports the Huanan market as the epicenter of the earliest documented COVID-19 outbreak.
reasons.I2: The evidence from both Worobey et al. (2022) and Pekar et al. (2022) supports zoonotic spillover as a more probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2's emergence than a non-zoonotic introduction.
reasons.I3: The evidence does not provide enough information to determine if the three Worobey proximity findings are statistically independent lines of evidence.
reasons.I4: The Pekar molecular two-lineage finding is based on genomic data, which is distinct from Worobey's market-proximity findings, allowing them to be treated as independent.
reasons.I5: The corpus does not provide enough information to justify reporting a single confident point-estimate odds ratio (on the order of 100:1) for zoonosis versus the alternative.
reasons.I6: The corpus evidence does not definitively rule out any non-market introduction of SARS-CoV-2 into humans.

reasons.F1: Worobey et al. (2022) explicitly state that the earliest known December-2019 COVID-19 cases were geographically centered on the Huanan market, including cases without direct market links.
reasons.F2: Worobey et al. (2022) report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
reasons.F3: Worobey et al. (2022) report that SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples inside the market were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.
reasons.F4: Worobey et al. (2022) state there is insufficient evidence to define upstream events, including the specific animal source of SARS-CoV-2.
reasons.F5: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) arising from at least two separate cross-species transmission events.
reasons.F6: Pekar et al. (2022) estimate the first zoonotic transmission (lineage B) occurred around mid-to-late November 2019.
reasons.F7: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude it is unlikely that SARS-CoV-2 circulated widely in humans before November 2019.
reasons.F8: The corpus does not report direct genomic sequencing identifying the specific intermediate animal host.
reasons.I1: The corpus evidence, including geographic centering and environmental samples, supports the Huanan market as the epicenter of the earliest documented COVID-19 outbreak.
reasons.I2: The corpus evidence, including zoonotic transmission events and live-mammal sales, makes zoonotic spillover more probable than non-zoonotic introduction.
reasons.I3: The three Worobey findings (geographic centering, live-mammal sales, environmental-sample association) are statistically independent lines of evidence.
reasons.I4: The Pekar molecular two-lineage finding is based on genomic data, distinct from Worobey's market-proximity findings, allowing them to be treated as independent.
reasons.I5: The corpus does not provide sufficient evidence to justify a single confident point-estimate odds ratio for zoonosis versus the alternative.
reasons.I6: The corpus evidence is not sufficient to definitively rule out any non-market introduction of SARS-CoV-2 into humans.

reasons.F1: Worobey et al. (2022) explicitly state that the earliest known December-2019 COVID-19 cases, including those without reported direct links, were geographically centered on the Huanan market.
reasons.F2: Worobey et al. (2022) report that live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were sold at the Huanan market in late 2019.
reasons.F3: Worobey et al. (2022) report that SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples within the market were spatially associated with vendors selling live mammals.
reasons.F4: Worobey et al. (2022) state that there is insufficient evidence to define upstream events, including the specific animal source of SARS-CoV-2.
reasons.F5: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that SARS-CoV-2's early genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages (A and B) resulting from at least two separate cross-species transmission events.
reasons.F6: Pekar et al. (2022) estimate the first (lineage B) zoonotic transmission occurred around 18 November 2019 (with a range of 23 October to 8 December).
reasons.F7: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude that it is unlikely that SARS-CoV-2 circulated widely in humans before November 2019.
reasons.F8: The corpus sources do not report direct genomic sequencing that identifies the specific intermediate animal host from which SARS-CoV-2 spilled over.
reasons.I1: Worobey et al. (2022) explicitly state that the Huanan market was the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic, supported by geographic and environmental evidence.
reasons.I2: Both Worobey et al. (2022) and Pekar et al. (2022) indicate that zoonotic spillover is the more probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2's emergence, with evidence of live mammal sales and multiple cross-species transmission events.
reasons.I3: The Evidence does not explicitly state whether the three Worobey proximity findings are statistically independent or whether their likelihood ratios may be multiplied.
reasons.I4: The Pekar molecular findings (genomic lineages) and Worobey's market-proximity findings are based on distinct datasets (genomic vs. epidemiological/environmental), so they may be treated as independent.
reasons.I5: The corpus does not provide sufficient evidence to justify reporting a single confident point-estimate odds ratio (e.g., 100:1) for zoonosis versus the alternative.
reasons.I6: The corpus evidence does not definitively rule out non-market introduction of SARS-CoV-2, as upstream events and exact circumstances remain obscure.

reasons.F1: Worobey et al. (2022) directly state this.
reasons.F2: Worobey et al. (2022) directly state this.
reasons.F3: Worobey et al. (2022) directly state this.
reasons.F4: Worobey et al. (2022) state they lacked sufficient evidence to define upstream events.
reasons.F5: Pekar et al. (2022) directly state this.
reasons.F6: Pekar et al. (2022) directly state this.
reasons.F7: Pekar et al. (2022) conclude the opposite, that it was unlikely SARS-CoV-2 was circulating widely before November 2019.
reasons.F8: The corpus does not report any direct genomic sequencing identifying the specific intermediate animal host.
reasons.I1: The Worobey findings strongly support the Huanan market as the epicenter.
reasons.I2: The two-lineage finding supports zoonotic spillover as a more probable explanation.
reasons.I3: The three Worobey findings are presented as independent observations.
reasons.I4: The Worobey and Pekar findings are based on distinct data.
reasons.I5: While the evidence is strong, it does not justify a precise odds ratio estimate.
reasons.I6: The corpus does not rule out all non-market introductions.
